title: Billiards in Polygons
speaker: Jonathan Chaika
abstract:
Consider a point mass traveling in a polygon. It travels in a straight line, with constant speed, until it hits a side, at which point it obeys the rules of elastic collision. What can we say about this? When all the angles of the polygon are rational multiples of pi, the travel of any trajectory is trapped in an invariant surface and we know a lot about it. In the case when at least one of the angles is irrational, it is much less understood, though from approximating with the rational case we know a couple of things. Kerckhoff, Masur and Smillie proved that there exists a billiard in an irrational polygon where the billiard flow is 'ergodic' with respect to the natural measure. This means that the amount of time the typical trajectory spends in a given box in the table (or even a cube in the three dimensional unit tangent bundle) is proportional to its area (or volume). This talk will present two results, both concerning a strengthening of ergodicity called ‘weak mixing’:
1) A strengthening of Kerckhoff, Masur and Smillie’s result: There exists a polygon where billiard flow is weakly mixing with respect to the natural volume on the unit tangent bundle.
2) A classification of the rational polygons where the billiard flow is weakly mixing with respect to the natural area on the invariant surfaces in the unit tangent bundle.
This talk will introduce ergodic theory and weak mixing and connect billiards in rational polygons to translation surfaces. Open questions will be presented and no previous knowledge of billiards, ergodic theory nor translation surfaces will be assumed.

proof_stages.label: Ergodic-theoretic framework and weak mixing criteria
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines probability-measure-preserving flows, ergodicity, the ergodic theorem, the ergodic hierarchy, and several equivalent formulations of weak mixing, especially product ergodicity and absence of nontrivial eigenfunctions.

proof_stages.label: Polygonal billiard flow and known baseline results
proof_stages.summary: The billiard flow in a polygon is defined on the quotient unit tangent bundle with its natural volume, and the speaker surveys what is known and unknown about entropy, periodic orbits, ergodicity, and minimality.

proof_stages.label: Rational-angle obstruction by reflection unfolding
proof_stages.summary: Reflecting the polygon rather than the trajectory shows that rational polygons have a finite reflection group and hence finitely many possible directions, obstructing ergodicity for the full three-dimensional volume but producing invariant area surfaces.

proof_stages.label: Translation surfaces as the invariant and ambient objects
proof_stages.summary: The unfolded rational billiard is interpreted as a translation surface, the unit tangent bundle is foliated by direction-dependent invariant translation surfaces, and these surfaces are placed inside strata with natural local Euclidean measures.

proof_stages.label: Product ergodicity for typical invariant surfaces of rational billiards
proof_stages.summary: The first main theorem states that for any rational polygon, for almost every pair of directions the diagonal flow on the product of the corresponding invariant translation surfaces is ergodic, providing the product-ergodicity input needed for weak mixing.

proof_stages.label: Affine circle-factor obstruction and rational-polygon classification
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains why almost-every-direction weak mixing on a fixed translation surface is false in general, identifies affine maps to the circle as the obstruction, and states the resulting classification of rational polygons whose invariant-surface flows are typically weakly mixing.

proof_stages.label: Baire-category transfer to irrational polygons with volume weak mixing
proof_stages.summary: Using the Kerckhoff–Masur–Smillie strategy, approximate ergodicity is made open, rational-polygon typical ergodicity/product ergodicity gives dense open conditions, and Baire category yields irrational polygons whose full billiard flow is ergodic or weakly mixing with respect to volume.

proof_stages.label: Limitations and further dynamical questions
proof_stages.summary: The closing discussion asks for explicit irrational examples, rates of weak mixing, mild mixing and rigidity questions, exceptional-set bounds, unique ergodicity of products, perturbations of speed, and higher-dimensional analogues.

examples.note: The square billiard is used to make concrete why periodic orbits in polygonal billiards may occur in uncountable parallel families even though the set of such families is countable.

examples.note: A particular rational polygon whose reflection group has eight orientations illustrates the rule 'reflect the table, not the trajectory' and why rational angles trap the billiard in finitely many directions.

examples.name: Height eigenfunction on a flat surface
examples.note: A pictured flat surface with a vertical-coordinate map to the circle shows concretely how an affine circle factor supplies eigenfunctions and prevents weak mixing in almost every direction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks a specific height-based function as an eigenfunction, explaining how it wraps and how the eigenvalue changes with direction
